easy.question: Which word is a coordinating conjunction?

data.explanation: “Until” is a subordinating conjunction showing time.
data.statement: I will stay until you return.
She will either come now or later.
He failed because he did not study.

easy.question: She passed the test {because} she studied.
Which word can mean 'time' as a subordinating conjunction?

data.explanation: It is a paired correlative conjunction used together.

easy.question: “Not only…but also” is an example of:

data.statement: We can go by car or by train.

easy.question: {Either} you come now {or} you miss the train.
He ran fast {so} he reached on time.

data.statement: Not only did she sing, but also she danced.
He is wiser than he looks.

easy.question: Which conjunction should NOT be used alone when pairing with 'neither'?
Which conjunction introduces a result or consequence?
He will go {if} you invite him.

data.statement: He spoke loudly so I could hear him.

easy.question: {Not only} was he late, {but also} he forgot his notes.

data.statement: While I waited, it began to rain.

easy.question: Which is an alternative/disjunctive coordinating conjunction?

data.statement: She studied hard, yet she failed.

easy.question: I will call you {when} I arrive.

data.statement: You will succeed if you work hard.

easy.question: Which is correct: 'He left ____ he was sick.'

hard.question: {No sooner} had she reached home {than} it started to rain.
Choose the best completion: "Scarcely had she sat down ____ the guests arrived."

data.statement: He tiptoed lest he wake the baby.

hard.question: Pick conjunction(s) that show negative purpose.
He spoke softly {lest} the baby wake.

data.statement: No sooner had we arrived than it began to rain.

hard.question: Pick conjunction(s).

data.explanation: “Than” can introduce a clause or a phrase.
options: It always requires a finite clause.
It can be followed by a clause or a phrase (elliptical).

hard.question: Which is correct about "than" in comparisons?

options: He spoke loudly lest he be heard.
He left early lest to miss the bus.
He left lest because he was late.
Lest he goes, we will wait.

hard.question: Which sentence uses "lest" correctly?

data.explanation: Verb should agree with nearer subject ('friends' → were).

hard.question: Identify the error: "Neither he nor his friends was present." The error is:

data.statement: She is not only intelligent but also hardworking.

hard.question: Pick conjunction pair requiring parallel structure.
She failed, {not because} she didn't try, {but because} she was ill.
Which correlative pair must be paralleled grammatically?

data.statement: Since he was ill, he stayed home.

hard.question: Pick conjunction used to introduce a cause.
{Scarcely} had I opened the door {when} the dog barked.
I would have helped you {if} I had known.

data.explanation: Correct pairing should be 'Scarcely...when' or 'No sooner...than'.
data.statement: Scarce had he left than the phone rang.

hard.question: Pick conjunction that should not be followed incorrectly.
Choose the best conjunction: "He would have passed ____ he studied harder."

data.statement: Were he to come, I would be pleased.

hard.question: Pick conjunction.
He behaved well, {although} he felt miserable.
In the sentence "Hardly had I entered the room when the lights went out," the conjunction is:

data.explanation: “Except that” introduces a clause of exception.
options: "Except that" can introduce a clause meaning 'but for the fact that'.

hard.question: Which is true about "except" vs "except that"?

data.statement: She reads more quickly than he does.

hard.question: Pick the comparative conjunction.
{Were} he here, I {would} ask him.

data.statement: Both the manager and the team agreed.

hard.question: Pick correlative pair.
I won the prize {even though} I had little time to prepare.

options: Though he was poor, he was cheerful.
Because he was poor, he was cheerful.
So he was poor that he was cheerful.
If he was poor, he was cheerful.

hard.question: Choose the correct transform: Combine with correct subordinating conjunction — "He was poor. He was cheerful."

data.statement: He was so exhausted that he slept immediately.

hard.question: Pick conjunction introducing result.
Identify most precise: "She declined, ____ she was tired."
He is richer {than} he appears.

data.statement: He will go, except that he may be tired.

hard.question: Pick conjunction pair expressing exception.
{Except that} he was tired, he would have come.

data.explanation: “When” indicates time/certainty for a general truth.

medium.question: Choose the best conjunction to complete: "____ you heat water, it boils."

data.statement: You will fail unless you try.

medium.question: I left early {because} I was unwell.

data.explanation: Standard order: 'not only... but also' with parallel structure.
options: He is not only clever but also hardworking.
He is only not clever but hardworking.
He not only is clever, but also hardworking.
Not only he is clever but also hardworking.

medium.question: Which sentence correctly uses correlative conjunctions?
{Unless} you hurry, you will miss the bus.

data.statement: He will both sing and dance.
Since you asked, I will tell you.

medium.question: Pick the best conjunction: "I will come, ____ I am invited."
{As} he was late, we started without him.

data.statement: We stayed because it rained.

options: It can introduce subordinate clauses in comparisons.
It's only used as a preposition.

medium.question: Do it {so that} you can finish on time.

data.statement: He cannot go unless he gets permission.

medium.question: {No sooner} had he sat down {than} the phone rang.
He stayed up late, {yet} he woke early.

data.statement: She neither called nor texted.
I prefer tea rather than coffee.

options: It requires inversion in main clause.

medium.question: Which is true about "no sooner... than":

data.statement: Where there is smoke, there is fire.
Either you study hard or you fail.
He was so tired that he slept.

medium.question: They went out {while} it was raining.
Which is correct: "She sings well, ____ she doesn't practice." (to show contrast)
